messages.content: {"success": true, "name": "nextjs-deployment-debugging", "description": "Diagnose and fix Next.js deployment issues — build failures, route registration, standalone output, Docker builds, and runtime routing.", "tags": ["nextjs", "deployment", "docker", "debugging", "build", "routes", "pages-router", "app-router"], "related_skills": ["systematic-debugging", "ecommerce-codebase-audit", "kanban-orchestrator"], "content": "--- name: nextjs-deployment-debugging description: "Diagnose and fix Next.js deployment issues — build failures, route registration, standalone output, Docker builds, and runtime routing." version: 1.0.0 author: Hermes Agent license: MIT metadata: hermes: tags: [nextjs, deployment, docker, debugging, build, routes, pages-router, app-router] related_skills: [systematic-debugging, ecommerce-codebase-audit, kanban-orchestrator] --- # Next.js Deployment & Debugging ## Overview This skill covers debugging and fixing Next.js deployment issues: build failures, routes not registering, standalone output problems, Docker build issues with GitHub Packages, and hybrid App Router / Pages Router troubleshooting. ### `pages/` and `app/` Directory Conflict When switching from App Router to Pages Router, a **stale root-level `app/` directory** (not `src/app/`) will cause: ``` pages and app directories should be under the same folder ``` **Fix:** Remove the stale `app/` directory at the repo root: ```bash rm -rf app ``` Always check for both `src/app/` AND `app/` when cleaning up. ## Working Patterns for Section-Based Content Sites When building a site that serves JSON-driven sectioned content from `getServerSideProps`, use this architecture: ### File structure ``` src/pages/index.tsx ← home page (SSR via getServerSideProps) src/pages/[slug].tsx ← all other pages (SSR via getServerSideProps) src/pages/admin/content.tsx ← admin editor (static, uses client-kit) src/pages/_document.tsx ← custom HTML shell src/pages/globals.css ← tailwind-based theme (not _app.tsx styling) src/components/sections.tsx ← all visual section components src/components/Header.tsx ← navigation bar src/components/Footer.tsx ← footer src/components/content.ts ← content resolver helper ``` ### Component section mapping pattern Each section in the page config JSON (e.g. `{id:"hero",variant:"split",content:"home.hero"}`) maps to a component via a `SECTION_MAP` record: ```tsx const SECTION_MAP: Record = { 'hero': HeroSection, 'stats-counter': StatsSection, 'trust-signals': TrustSection, 'programs-comparison': ProgramsSection, 'services': ServicesSection, 'tax-savings-calculator': TaxCalculatorSection, 'why-destination': WhyCountrySection, 'features': FeaturesSection, 'process-timeline': ProcessSection, 'testimonials': TestimonialsSection, 'cta-banner': CtaBanner, } ``` ### Data flow: pageContent shape MUST match component keys **CRITICAL:** The `pageContent` object passed to each component must have keys matching what the component looks up. The standard pattern: ```tsx // In the dispatch (pages/[slug].tsx or pages/index.tsx): const sectionData = resolveContent(content, section.content || section.id) const pc: any = {} const keyName = section.content?.split('.').pop() || section.id // "home.hero" → "hero" if (section.content || section.id) pc[keyName] = sectionData || content return ``` This creates `{hero: {...}}` from section.content=`"home.hero"`, matching what `HeroSection` expects (`pageContent.hero`). **Common failure modes:** 1. **section.id used as key instead of section.content suffix:** If the dispatch uses `pc[section.id]` (e.g. `pc["programs-comparison"]`) but the component looks for `pageContent.programs`, the data is stored under a key the component can't find. Always extract the last segment of the `section.content` dotpath. 2. **pageContent is the raw sectionData, not wrapped:** If you pass `pageContent = sectionData` directly (the resolved object), then `HeroSection` does `pageContent.hero` which is `undefined` because `pageContent` IS the hero object. The old `buildPageContent()` pattern wrapped it as `{hero: {...}}`. 3. **Components reading `pageContent` directly instead of via `.KEY`:** New components (in sections-extra.tsx) use `const d = data || pageContent || {}` where `data` is the raw section data passed as a separate prop. This is the preferred pattern. Older components (in sections.tsx) use `const c = pageContent.KEY || {}` which relies on the wrapping. Both work as long as the dispatch creates the correct shape. ### Brand tokens (CSS custom properties) Always apply brand colors as inline styles or CSS variables — Tailwind `@theme` directives in `globals.css` are ignored by Pages Router: ```css /* src/pages/globals.css */ @import "tailwindcss"; @theme { --color-primary: #1B2A4A; --color-secondary: #C9A96E; --color-accent: #C9A96E; --color-background: #FFFFFF; --color-surface: #FFFFFF; --color-surface-alt: #F5F5F0; --color-text: #1B2A4A; --color-text-muted: #5C6B7A; --color-border: #E0E0E0; --color-success: #10b981; } ``` Then in components, reference inline: `#1B2A4A` for navy, `#C9A96E` for gold, `#F5F5F0` for light backgrounds. ### SEO per page For pages, load SEO data from `content.es.json` at the matching page key path: ```tsx const seo = resolveContent(content, `${pageId}Page.seo`) || resolveContent(content, `${pageId}.seo`) || pageConfig?.seoTitle || {} ``` Set `` and `<meta name="description">` from this data. ### Content shape reference for section components The `content/es.json` in this project has this nesting: ```json { "siteName": "...", "tagline": "...", "navigation": { "navItems": [...], "ctaText": "...", "ctaHref": "..." }, "home": { "hero": { "headline": "...", "subheadline": "...", "ctaPrimaryText": "...", "ctaPrimaryHref": "...", "ctaSecondaryText": "...", "trustBadges": [...] }, "stats": { "items": [{ "value": "...", "label": "...", "icon": "..." }] }, "trust": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "items": [{ "icon": "...", "title": "...", "description": "...", "image": "..." }] }, "programs": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "tiers": [{ "id": "...", "name": "...", "price": "...", "included": [...], "highlighted": bool }] }, "services": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "groups": [{ "title": "...", "items": [{ "title": "...", "description": "..." }] }] }, "whyCountry": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "pillars": [{ "title": "...", "description": "...", "bullets": [...] }] }, "process": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "steps": [{ "number": int, "title": "...", "description": "...", "duration": "..." }] }, "testimonials": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "items": [{ "author": "...", "role": "...", "quote": "...", "rating": int }] }, "finalCta": { "title": "...", "subtitle": "...", "buttonText": "...", "buttonHref": "..." }, "taxCalculator": { "title": "...", "subtitle": "..." }, "beneluxDesk": { "title": "...", "subtitle": "...", "items": [{ "title": "...", "description": "..." }] } }, "footer": { "businessName": "...", "columns": [{ "title": "...", "links": [{ "label": "...", "href": "..." }] }], "copyright": "..." }, "whatsapp": "595...", "contactPage": { "hero": {...}, "contact": {...} }, "faqPage": { "full": { "items": [{ "q": "...", "a": "..." }] } }, "aboutPage": { "hero": {...}, "story": { "paragraphs": [...] }, "team": { "members": [{ "name": "...", "role": "..." }] }, "gallery": { "images": [{"$img":"..."}] } }, // ... 20+ more page content entries } ``` ### Brand tokens (from tokens.json) ```json { "primary": "#1B2A4A", // navy — headers, backgrounds, buttons "secondary": "#C9A96E", // gold — CTAs, accents, highlights "accent": "#C9A96E", "background": "#FFFFFF", "surface": "#FFFFFF", "surfaceLight": "#EDE8DB", // caramel tint "text": "#1B2A4A", "textLight": "#333333", "textMuted": "#6B6B6B" } ``` ## Turbopack + file: Dependencies: Symlink Resolution Failure When a `package.json` uses `file:` links for local packages (e.g. `"@ai-whisperers/sections": "file:../base/packages/sections"`), npm install creates a **symlink** in `node_modules/`. Turbopack (Next.js 16+) does NOT follow these symlinks — the build fails with: ``` Module not found: Can't resolve '@ai-whisperers/sections' ``` This happens even with `transpilePackages: ['@ai-whisperers/sections']` at top-level in `next.config.js`. **Root cause:** Turbopack uses its own module resolution that doesn't traverse `file:` symlinks. It's a confirmed behavior, not a config fix. **Three workarounds:** ### Workaround A: Copy script (recommended for local dev) **CRITICAL: When the site repo has a `packages/@scope/sections/src/` directory AND `node_modules/@scope/sections/dist/`, Turbopack resolves from `src/` not `dist/` — even when `package.json` exports point to `./dist/index.js`.** To fully resolve, copy BOTH source and compiled output before the Docker build: ```bash cp -r ../ai-whisperers-base/packages/sections/src/* packages/@ai-whisperers/sections/src/ cp -r ../ai-whisperers-base/packages/sections/dist/* packages/@ai-whisperers/sections/dist/ ``` Create a script that replaces the symlink with a real copy before build: ```javascript // scripts/copy-package.cjs const fs = require('fs') const path = require('path') const pkgJson = JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync('package.json', 'utf8')) const link = pkgJson.dependencies['@ai-whisperers/sections'] if (!link || !link.startsWith('file:')) process.exit(0) const targetPath = path.resolve('node_modules/@ai-whisperers/sections') if (!fs.existsSync(targetPath)) process.exit(0) let stat try { stat = fs.lstatSync(targetPath) } catch { process.exit(0) } if (!stat.isSymbolicLink()) { process.exit(0) } let realPath try { realPath = fs.readlinkSync(targetPath) } catch { process.exit(0) } const absRealPath = path.resolve(path.dirname(targetPath), realPath) if (!fs.existsSync(absRealPath)) process.exit(1) fs.rmSync(targetPath, { recursive: true, force: true }) ;(function copyRecursive(src, dest) { if (!fs.existsSync(dest)) fs.mkdirSync(dest, { recursive: true }) for (const entry of fs.readdirSync(src, { withFileTypes: true })) { const s = path.join(src, entry.name), d = path.join(dest, entry.name) entry.isDirectory() ? copyRecursive(s, d) : fs.copyFileSync(s, d) } })(absRealPath, targetPath) ``` Add to package.json scripts: ```json { "scripts": { "build": "node scripts/copy-package.cjs && next build", "postinstall": "node scripts/copy-package.cjs" } } ``` Note: `postinstall` runs during `npm install` but the target symlink may not exist yet (npm creates it after the postinstall lifecycle). The script handles this gracefully with an early exit. The `build` script always works because `npm install` ran first. ### Workaround B: Pre-install + copy on host (for Docker) When the base package is also on the VPS, pre-copy it before Docker build: ```bash cp -r /root/base/packages/sections node_modules/@ai-whisperers/sections # Then docker build without the file: dep ``` ### Workaround C: Avoid file: links entirely — publish to npm/GHCR The permanent fix. Publish the package to npm or GitHub Packages, reference it by version: ```json "@ai-whisperers/sections": "^0.1.0" ``` This eliminates the symlink entirely — Turbopack resolves it as a normal npm package. Requires an npm publish workflow. See `references/turbopack-file-link-symlink-resolution.md` for the full session transcript with what was tried and what worked. ## Critical Knowledge: Next.js 16 Turbopack Bug **Next.js 16.2.4 with Turbopack has a confirmed bug where root App Router pages are silently dropped from the route table.** The following patterns produce NO route output: - `src/app/page.tsx` — root page is compiled (shows 4/4 in build) but produces NO `page.js` file and is NOT in routes-manifest - `src/app/[[...slug]]/page.tsx` — catch-all routes are completely ignored by Turbopack - `src/app/index/page.tsx` — same behavior, no route registered - `src/app/home/page.tsx` — same behavior **Only these routes work in App Router mode:** - `/_not-found` (handled internally) - `/_global-error` (handled internally) - Any explicit named route like `/admin/content` (works fine) - API routes like `/api/admin/content` (work fine) **Root cause:** Turbopack in Next.js 16 silently drops root-level pages that use synchronous filesystem I/O (`readFileSync`, `readdirSync`) or async patterns — even trivial `<h1>Hello</h1>` pages. The bug is in Turbopack's route discovery, not the build output. ## Workaround: Switch to Pages Router When App Router root pages fail to register: 1. **Delete `src/app/`** entirely (App Router and Pages Router can't coexist in Next.js 16) 2. **Create `src/pages/index.tsx`** using `getServerSideProps` (Pages Router SSR): ```tsx // src/pages/index.tsx import { readFileSync } from 'fs' import path from 'path' export default function Home({ content }: any) { return <main>...</main> } export function getServerSideProps() { const data = JSON.parse(readFileSync(path.join(process.cwd(), 'content', 'es.json'), 'utf-8')) return { props: { content: data } } } ``` 3. **Move other App Router pages** to Pages Router: - `src/app/admin/content/page.tsx` → `src/pages/admin/content.tsx` (static or dynamic) - `src/app/api/admin/content/route.ts` → N/A — Pages Router uses API routes differently 4. **Convert `next.config.ts` to `next.config.js`** — Pages-only mode doesn't support .ts config files ### Build output for Pages Router (working) ``` Route (pages) ┌ ƒ / ← Dynamic SSR page ├ ○ /404 ← Static error page ├ ○ /admin/content ← Static admin page ƒ Proxy (Middleware) ← Request logging ``` ## Docker Build with GitHub Packages ### Issue: `npm ci` fails with lock file mismatch When `package-lock.json` has `@ai-whisperers/*` packages but `package.json` doesn't specify versions, `npm ci` errors: ``` Invalid: lock file's @ai-whisperers/admin@0.2.1 does not satisfy @ai-whisperers/admin@ ``` **Fix:** Use `npm install --legacy-peer-deps` instead of `npm ci` in the Dockerfile. ### Issue: npm install fails inside Docker for GitHub Packages ``` npm error 404 Not Found - GET https://registry.npmjs.org/@ai-whisperers%2fclient-kit - Not found ``` **Fix:** The Dockerfile's deps stage must COPY `.npmrc`: ```dockerfile COPY package.json package-lock.json* .npmrc ./ RUN npm install --legacy-peer-deps ``` Without `.npmrc`, the Docker build can't reach `npm.pkg.github.com`. ### Issue: Content/static files missing in Docker image Even with `COPY --from=builder` in the Dockerfile, `content/` and `nexa-pages/` directories may be missing in the runner. **The `npm ci` failure causes the builder stage to fail silently** (Docker caches the previous successful build, so `COPY` from the failed builder stage is skipped, reverting to the cached runner stage). **Fix:** Always verify content files exist in the final image: ```bash docker run --rm my-image:prod ls -la /app/content ``` ## Tests for Content/Data Loading in SSR ### Live content audit script When a site serves HTTP 200 but only shows `<h1>` with no actual sections, create a standalone audit script: ```python #!/usr/bin/env python3 import json, urllib.request # Check content resolution c = json.load(open('content/es.json')) print('Content keys:', list(c.keys())) print('Home keys:', list(c['home'].keys())) # Check page config sections p = json.load(open('nexa-pages/home.json')) for s in p['sections']: key = s.get('content') or s.get('id') sc = c.get(key) print(f' Section {s[\"id\"]}: key={key} direct={!!sc}') if not sc and key and '.' in key: sc = key.split('.').reduce(lambda o,k: o.get(k) if o else None, c) print(f' dot-path={!!sc}') # Check live site h = urllib.request.urlopen('https://example.com/').read().decode() print(f'Size: {len(h)}') print(f'Has hero: {\"headline\" in h}') ``` ### Dot-notation content resolution pattern Content in `content/es.json` is nested (e.g. `c.home.hero.headline`), but section configs use dot-notation strings like `"home.hero"`. The SSR component must resolve via path traversal: ```tsx // WRONG — returns null because "home.hero" is not a flat key let sc = content?.["home.hero"]; // RIGHT — traverses home → hero → object let sc = "home.hero".split('.').reduce((o: any, k: string) => o?.[k], content); ``` Always include this helper in Pages Router SSR components: ```tsx function resolveContent(content: any, key: string): any { if (!content || !key) return null let sc = content[key] if (!sc && key.includes('.')) { sc = key.split('.').reduce((o: any, k: string) => o?.[k], content) } return sc } ``` ### Testing HTTP from inside a container BusyBox `wget` in Alpine containers can return `Connection refused` even when the HTTP server IS listening. Use Node.js TCP socket instead: ```bash docker exec $CID node -e " const http = require('http'); http.get({hostname:'localhost',port:3000,path:'/'}, r => { let d = ''; r.on('data', c => d += c); r.on('end', () => console.log(r.statusCode, d.length, 'bytes')); }).on('error', e => console.log('ERR:', e.message)); setTimeout(() => process.exit(), 3000); " ``` ## File Corruption: `patch` Tool + read_file View Pitfall **The `patch` tool writes the raw file content from the conversation context.** If the file was previously read with `read_file` (which formats output with line-number prefixes like ` 1|{`), those prefixes ARE NOT in the file on disk — they're display formatting injected by the tool. But if you use `execute_code` with a `write_file()` call that passes the read_file output string directly, the line-number prefixes get written into the file, corrupting it. **Symptoms:** - `JSONDecodeError: Extra data: line 1 column 7 (char 6)` — the `1|` prefix makes JSON invalid - Git shows `1|{` as the first line of the file - The file appears in `git log` with `+501 -501` or similar large diffs **How it happens:** ```python # WRONG — read_file's display format includes line numbers content = read_file("images.json") # Returns {"content": " 1|{\n 2| ..."} write_file("images.json", content["content"]) # Writes the line-number prefix! ``` **Fix:** 1. Restore from git: `git checkout -- <file>` 2. Or restore from a known-good source copy 3. Re-apply edits using `patch` (which operates on the actual file on disk, not the display format) **Best practice for editing JSON files:** - Use `patch` for surgical replacements (it modifies the file on disk directly) - Use Python scripts with `json.load` / `json.dump` for bulk transformations - NEVER pipe read_file output back into write_file **Verification after editing:** ```bash python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('file.json')); print('Valid ✓')" ``` ## Standalone Repo Divergence: Content Path Fixes When a standalone site repo is forked from a multi-tenant builder (e.g. `paragu-ai-builder` → `nexa-paraguay`), the content JSON files retain multi-tenant paths that break in standalone mode. ### Paths that must be fixed | Multi-tenant path | Standalone fix | |---|---| | `/s/es/nexa-paraguay/programas` | `/programas` | | `/s/nl/nexa-paraguay/contacto` | `/contacto` | | `/s/de/nexa-paraguay/benelux` | `/benelux` | | `/sites/nexa-paraguay/images/logo.webp` | `/images/logo.webp` | ### Where these paths hide 1. **Navigation hrefs** — `navigation.navItems[].href` — every nav link 2. **CTA buttons** — `home.hero.ctaPrimaryHref`, `home.finalCta.buttonHref` 3. **Program tier CTAs** — `home.programs.tiers[].ctaHref` 4. **Footer links** — `footer.columns[].links[].href` 5. **Image manifests** — `images.json` → `basePath`, every `src` and `fallbackSrc` 6. **Testimonials** — `testimonials.json` → `image`, `videoPoster` 7. **Content file image references** — only if they use `/sites/nexa-paraguay/images/` directly ### How to search ```bash grep -rn "/s/es/nexa-paraguay/" content/ # locale paths grep -rn "/sites/nexa-paraguay/images/" content/ images.json # image paths ``` Count them: ```bash grep -c "/s/es/nexa-paraguay" content/es.json # should be 0 in standalone ``` ### Dockerfile missing runtime data files When a Next.js Pages Router site loads data from `getServerSideProps` via `readFileSync('images.json')`, the Dockerfile's runtime stage MUST include that file: ```dockerfile # WRONG — images.json not copied, runtime returns null for ALL image refs COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/public ./public COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/content ./content # RIGHT — all runtime data files included COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/public ./public COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/images.json ./images.json # ← REQUIRED COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/content ./content COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/nexa-pages ./nexa-pages ``` **Symptom of missing data files:** Site loads with HTTP 200, has correct header/footer/nav, but `<img>` tags resolve to empty alt-only elements. No error in logs because `loadJSON` returns `{}` instead of throwing. **Verification:** Check inside the running container: ```bash docker exec $CONTAINER ls -la /app/images.json # If "No such file or directory", the file wasn't copied in Dockerfile ``` ### Pages Router: If all pages show HTTP 200 but section content is missing Check three things: 1. **Page config has the right `sections` array** — each section needs `id` and `content` fields referencing paths that exist in `content/es.json` 2. **SLUG_MAP has an entry** — every `nexa-pages/*.json` file needs a corresponding entry in `SLUG_MAP` in `[slug].tsx`. If the slug isn't in the map, the page returns 404. 3. **Rich fallback renderer** — the generic section renderer in `[slug].tsx` must handle more than just `headline`/`subheadline`. It should also render: - `eyebrow` — small uppercase label - `body` or `content` — plain text paragraphs split by `\n` - `data.items`, `data.groups`, `data.pillars`, `data.full.items` — arrays rendered as card grids - Items support: `title/pregunta/question/term` + `description/respuesta/answer/definition/body` `docker stack deploy` can silently keep the old container running if the image tag hasn't changed. Docker doesn't re-pull or rebuild by tag; it only creates a new task when the digest changes. **Symptom:** You pushed code, rebuilt the Docker image, redeployed, but the old content still shows. **Fix: Force update the service** ```bash docker service update --force <service_name> ``` **Verify the new container is running:** ```bash docker service ps <service_name> # Check CURRENT STATE shows "Running X seconds ago", not "Running X hours ago" ``` **Verify content changes took effect:** ```bash curl -s https://site.com/ | grep "expected string" ``` Or check inside the container: ```bash docker exec $(docker ps --filter name=<service_name> -q) cat /app/content/es.json | head -5 ``` ## Testing Build Output ### Check which routes Next.js registered ```bash # Routes manifest (App Router) python3 -c "import json; d=json.load(open('.next/routes-manifest.json')); print([r['page'] for r in d.get('staticRoutes',[])])" # Prerender manifest (App Router) python3 -c "import json; d=json.load(open('.next/prerender-manifest.json')); print(list(d['routes'].keys()))" # Build output shows "Route (app)" or "Route (pages)" section ``` ### Check compiled page files ```bash find .next -name 'page.js' -type f | sort ``` If root pages are missing (no `page.js` for `/`), suspect the Turbopack bug. ### Test standalone server ```bash cd .next/standalone && PORT=3000 HOSTNAME=0.0.0.0 node server.js & sleep 5 && curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' http://localhost:3000/ ``` #### Healthcheck Pitfalls Next.js standalone startup prints "Ready in 0ms" BEFORE the HTTP server actually calls `accept()`. A healthcheck immediately after startup will cause the container to restart in a loop. **Don't use a healthcheck or use a generous start period:** ```yaml healthcheck: test: ["CMD", "wget", "-qO-", "http://localhost:3000/"] start_period: 30s interval: 30s ``` **Route selection for healthchecks:** Must ping a URL returning HTTP 200. Test locally first: ```bash docker run --rm -d -p 13000:3000 <image> && sleep 5 && curl -I http://localhost:13000/<route> ``` Common pitfalls: - Root `/` may return 404 with Next 16 Turbopack route group bug (`(store)/page.tsx`) - `/admin/content` is a good target if the admin panel loads - `/api/health` is ideal if the app exposes one - BusyBox `wget` returns `Connection refused` on Alpine even when ready BusyBox `wget` (Alpine) may get `Connection refused` even when the socket is ready. Use `node -e` to test HTTP internally instead: ```bash docker exec $CID node -e " const net = require('net'); const c = net.createConnection(3000, '127.0.0.1'); c.on('connect', () => { c.write('GET / HTTP/1.0\r\n\r\n'); }); c.on('data', d => console.log(d.toString().split('\r\n')[0])); setTimeout(() => process.exit(), 5000); " ``` ## GitHub Packages + Docker Build: Auth Workaround ### Issue: Docker build can't authenticate to npm.pkg.github.com When building a Next.js site with `@ai-whisperers/*` private packages, Docker build fails at `npm install` because it lacks a `GITHUB_TOKEN`: ``` npm error 401 Unauthorized - GET https://npm.pkg.github.com/@ai-whisperers%2fseo ``` Also: even when using `--build-arg GITHUB_TOKEN`, Docker's BuildKit security scanner blocks `ENV GITHUB_TOKEN` in the Dockerfile with: ``` SecretsUsedInArgOrEnv: Do not use ARG or ENV instructions for sensitive data ``` This means standard `docker build --build-arg` + `ENV` approach won't work with modern Docker. ### Solution A: Prebuild on host, use lightweight Docker image (RECOMMENDED) The `.npmrc` file reads `$GITHUB_TOKEN` from the environment, but Docker build can't access the host's environment. Instead: 1. Run `npm install && npm run build` on the host (VPS) where `GITHUB_TOKEN` is available 2. Use a separate production Dockerfile (`Dockerfile.prod`) that ONLY copies the already-built output — no npm install needed: ```dockerfile # Dockerfile.prod — use AFTER building on the host FROM node:20-alpine AS runner WORKDIR /app ENV NODE_ENV=production NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1 PORT=3000 HOSTNAME="0.0.0.0" RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs && adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs COPY --chown=nextjs:nodejs . . USER nextjs EXPOSE 3000 HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=10s --start-period=10s --retries=3 CMD wget --no-verbose --tries=1 --spider http://127.0.0.1:3000/ || exit 1 CMD ["node_modules/.bin/next", "start"] ``` **Important:** This Dockerfile uses `node_modules/.bin/next start` not the `server.js` from standalone mode, because the host build produces a `.next` directory suitable for `next start`. Deploy sequence: ```bash cd /root/pitchy-website git fetch origin && git reset --hard origin/main export GITHUB_TOKEN=$(gh auth token) npm ci --legacy-peer-deps npx next build docker build -f Dockerfile.prod -t pitchy:prod . docker service update pitchy_web --force --image pitchy:prod ``` ### Solution B: Docker build-time secret (for CI/CD) In GitHub Actions, pass the token as a Docker build secret: ```yaml - name: Build Docker run: | docker build \ --secret id=GITHUB_TOKEN,env=GITHUB_TOKEN \ -t mysite:prod . ``` Dockerfile: ```dockerfile RUN --mount=type=secret,id=GITHUB_TOKEN \ GITHUB_TOKEN=$(cat /run/secrets/GITHUB_TOKEN) npm install --legacy-peer-deps ``` ## SvelteKit Static Site Deployment (nginx + adapter-static) When deploying a SvelteKit site with `@sveltejs/adapter-static` behind nginx/Docker, the workflow differs from Next.js. ### Dockerfile (nginx alpine) ```dockerfile FROM nginx:alpine COPY build /usr/share/nginx/html COPY nginx.conf /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf ``` ### nginx.conf (SPA-friendly) ```nginx server { listen 80; server_name _; root /usr/share/nginx/html; index index.html; location / { try_files $uri $uri.html $uri/ =404; } location /_app/ { expires 1y; add_header Cache-Control "public, immutable"; } } ``` **Note:** `try_files $uri $uri.html` is critical — SvelteKit prerenders `/gallery` as `gallery.html`, so nginx must serve `$uri.html` as a fallback. ### Common Pitfall: Missing prerender entries **Symptom:** Page builds fine locally (`.svelte-kit/` has the route) but 404s in production. **Root cause 1 — prerender entries list:** The `svelte.config.js` `prerender.entries` array must explicitly list every route: ```js kit: { adapter: adapter({ pages: 'build', assets: 'build', fallback: '404.html' }), prerender: { entries: ['/', '/about', '/gallery', '/tickets', '/hotel', '/schedule', '/faq', '/rules', '/contact', '/login', '/privacy', '/dealers-den', '/robots.txt', '/sitemap.xml'], handleUnseenRoutes: 'ignore' } } ``` **Root cause 2 — missing `+page.ts`:** Even with the correct entries list, a route won't prerender without a `+page.ts` file exporting `prerender = true`: ```typescript // src/routes/gallery/+page.ts export const prerender = true; ``` **Verification:** Check `build/` directory for the page: ```bash ls build/gallery.html # must exist ``` ### Cross-Page Navigation Pattern for Multi-Page Sites For 10+ page SvelteKit static sites, ensure every page is reachable: 1. **Navbar** — list ALL pages (not just a subset). For anthro-party-argentina: Inicio, Entradas, Hotel, Cronograma, Dealers Den, Galería, Sobre APA!, FAQ, Reglamento, Contacto, Inscribite 2. **Footer** — organized columns by category (Evento, Información, Redes). Include sitemap.xml link. 3. **Cross-page links** at bottom of each page — 3 related pages per page with arrow indicators. For example: Tickets page links to Cronograma, Hotel, FAQ. Schedule links to Entradas, Dealers Den, Hotel. 4. **All images must have descriptive `alt` text** for accessibility. ### Svelte svelte.config.js ```js import adapter from '@sveltejs/adapter-static'; const config = { kit: { adapter: adapter({ pages: 'build', assets: 'build', fallback: '404.html' }), prerender: { origin: 'https://yourdomain.com', entries: ['/', ...allRoutes] } } }; ``` **Pitfall:** The `origin` in prerender config fixes the canonical URL leak (`http://sveltekit-prerender`). Without it, search engines see the build hostname. ## Docker Compose + Traefik: Label Placement Pitfall **The MOST COMMON cause of "works locally, 404 online"** after a Next.js fix is **Traefik labels not being applied to the Docker container**. ### The Bug In `docker-compose.yml`, labels under `deploy:` are **ignored by `docker compose up -d`** — they are only read by `docker stack deploy` (Swarm mode): ```yaml services: web: labels: # CORRECT — service-level labels work with both - "traefik.enable=true" - "traefik.http.routers.myapp.rule=Host(`example.com`)" deploy: labels: # WRONG — ignored by `docker compose up -d` - "traefik.enable=true" ``` ### How to verify labels are applied ```bash docker inspect $CONTAINER_ID --format '{{json .Config.Labels}}' | python3 -c ' import sys,json; l=json.load(sys.stdin) for k,v in sorted(l.items()): if "traefik" in k.lower(): print(k,"=",v) ' ``` If **only** `com.docker.compose.*` labels appear, Traefik labels weren't applied. ### The Fix 1. Move labels from `deploy:` to the service level in docker-compose.yml 2. Use `docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml $STACK_NAME` instead of `docker compose up -d` 3. Verify: `docker service ls` should show the service with labels applied ### CI/CD Secrets Access: Standalone vs Reusable Workflows When using GitHub Actions reusable workflows from `Ai-Whisperers/ci-cd`, the called workflow may NOT have access to the calling repo's org-level secrets (VPS_HOST, VPS_KEY, etc.), even with `secrets: inherit`. **Symptom:** CI run shows "This run likely failed because of a workflow file issue" with 0 jobs. **Fix:** Use a self-contained deploy workflow that doesn't depend on the `ci-cd` repo: ```yaml name: Deploy on: push: branches: [main] jobs: deploy: runs-on: ubuntu-latest timeout-minutes: 20 steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - uses: actions/setup-node@v4 with: node-version: '20' cache: 'npm' - name: Install dependencies run: npm ci --legacy-peer-deps - name: Build run: npm run build - name: Setup SSH run: | mkdir -p ~/.ssh echo "${{ secrets.VPS_KEY }}" > ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 ssh-keyscan -H ${{ secrets.VPS_HOST }} >> ~/.ssh/known_hosts 2>/dev/null || true - name: Deploy via SSH run: | ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new ${{ secrets.VPS_USER }}@${{ secrets.VPS_HOST }} \ "cd /root/mysite && \ git fetch origin && git reset --hard origin/main && \ export GITHUB_TOKEN=\$(gh auth token) && \ npm ci --legacy-peer-deps && npx next build && \ docker build -f Dockerfile.prod -t mysite:prod . && \ docker service update mysite_web --force --image mysite:prod" ``` Alternatively, deploy directly from the agent via SSH (`ssh root@VPS ...`) which is faster and doesn't depend on GitHub Actions secrets at all. ```bash # Test from inside Traefik container (on same network as service) docker exec $TRAEFIK_CONTAINER sh -c 'wget -q -O - --timeout=5 http://$SERVICE_NAME:3000/ 2>&1 | head -3' # Test via Traefik directly (bypassing Cloudflare) curl -s -o /dev/null -w 'HTTP %{http_code} size=%{size_download}' -H 'Host: example.com' http://localhost:80/ curl -s -o /dev/null -w 'HTTP %{http_code} size=%{size_download}' -H 'Host: example.com' --insecure https://localhost/ ``` ### Traffic flow diagram ``` Browser → Cloudflare (orange) → VPS:443 → Traefik → agent-net → container:3000 → Next.js ``` 404 at any point in this chain breaks the site. ### SSR Content Duplication: Empty Container Shell When investigating a suspected duplicate content section, **check whether the second "section" is actually an empty component** with no visible content rather than a true content duplicate. The CTA banner section (`cta-banner`) is the most common culprit — it renders a `style="padding:2rem 1rem;background:#F5F5F0"` container shell even when the content data resolves to `null` or empty. **Fix:** Add a null-guard in the section dispatch or the component itself: ```tsx // In the dispatch loop ([[...slug]].tsx): {sections.map((section, idx) => { const Component = SECTION_MAP[section.id] const sectionData = resolveContent(content, section.content) if (!sectionData || Object.keys(sectionData).length === 0) return null // ... render })} ``` For reference material on this debugging path, see `references/ssr-content-duplication-debugging.md`. ## PPR + 'use client' + dynamic(): Content in RSC Payload But Empty `<main>` **Symptom:** Page loads with HTTP 200. Header, footer, and nav render correctly. `<main></main>` is empty in the HTML. The full content JSON is present in `self.__next_f.push` RSC script tags (visible via curl). Content appears when JavaScript fully hydrates. **Root cause chain:** 1. The `SectionsRenderer` component is marked `'use client'` at the top of the file 2. Each section component is loaded via `next/dynamic` with `{ ssr: true }` 3. With PPR (`cacheComponents: true` in next.config.js), the route is `◐ Partial Prerender` 4. PPR builds a static HTML shell at build-time that contains an empty `<main>` container 5. The dynamic content streams via RSC payload into the script tags, then React hydrates and fills `<main>` on the client **This is NOT a bug — it's how Next.js App Router + PPR works.** The content renders correctly in the browser. But if the user checks the HTML source directly (curl, View Source), they see empty `<main>`. ### When to fix it Fix when: - Users check the page source and think the page is broken - SEO crawlers don't render JavaScript (Google usually does render JS, but no guarantee) - Slow connections where the RSC payload takes time to download and hydrate ### Fixes (in order of preference) **Option 1: Disable PPR entirely** (fastest, safest) ```js // next.config.js — remove cacheComponents: true const nextConfig = { // cacheComponents: true, ← DELETE THIS output: "standalone", } ``` Then remove any `<Suspense>` wrappers from page components: ```tsx // Before: <Suspense><SectionsRenderer ... /></Suspense> // After: <SectionsRenderer ... /> ``` All routes become `● SSG` (fully pre-rendered HTML). **Option 2: Remove 'use client' from SectionsRenderer** (more work, better SSR) Convert SectionsRenderer to a Server Component. Import section components directly instead of via `dynamic()`: ```tsx // Instead of: import dynamic from 'next/dynamic' const HeroSection = dynamic(() => import('./sections').then(m => ({ default: m.HeroSection })), { ssr: true }) // Use: import { HeroSection } from './sections' ``` **Caveat:** This breaks if the section components import `'use client'` features (hooks, browser APIs). Sections that need client interactivity must stay as client components. **Option 3: Accept the current behavior** (valid if you control the SEO narrative) Content is in the RSC payload and renders via JS hydration. Real browsers see it. The `<main>` being empty in raw HTML is an architectural choice, not a bug. ### Content Resolution for sections.tsx Components When sections use the `content: "home"` pattern in page configs: ```json // nexa-pages/home.json { "id": "hero", "content": "home" } { "id": "trust-signals", "content": "home" } ``` The `resolveContent(content, "home")` returns the `content.home` object. Components like `HeroSection` do `pageContent.hero` — they expect `pageContent` to be the `home` object so they can access sub-keys. **Fix when a section returns null despite content existing:** 1. Check what key the component accesses: `pageContent.hero`, `const d = data || pageContent`, `d.groups` 2. Ensure the `content` key resolves to the right depth: - `content: "home"` → for components that access sub-keys (`pageContent.hero`, `pageContent.trust`) - `content: "home.feedback"` → for components that use the resolved data directly (`data.title`) 3. For sections using `data || pageContent || {}` with `d.groups`, check if the data has `groups` at the expected path: ```tsx // Fix: add fallback path const groups = d.services?.groups || d.groups || [] ``` **Verification:** After any content key fix, rebuild and check: ```bash curl -sL https://site.com/es/ | grep -c "expected headline text" ``` ### Dynamic Import in Server Component Error If you import directly in a Server Component and hit `Class extends value undefined is not a constructor or null`: - The section files likely have `'use client'` at the top - `dynamic()` is the correct loading pattern for client components in Server Components - When removing PPR, keep `dynamic()` with `{ ssr: true }` — it still renders server-side HTML but handles the client boundary correctly - Only exit-popup type components need `{ ssr: false }` ### pageConfig Not Loaded for Home Page (slug = 'home' Guard Bug) **Symptom:** Home page returns HTTP 200, header/footer/nav render, but `<main>` is empty. All other pages (about, servicios, etc.) render full content correctly. **Root cause:** In `src/lib/page-data.ts`: ```typescript const pageId = slug === 'home' ? 'home' : slug if (slug !== 'home') { try { pageConfig = JSON.parse(readFileSync(...)) } catch { return null } } ``` For `slug = 'home'`, the `pageConfig` loading is entirely skipped — result is `pageConfig: null`. Sections map over `pageConfig?.sections || []` which resolves to `[].sections || []` = empty array. **Fix:** Remove the slug guard. Load pageConfig for ALL slugs including 'home': ```typescript const pageId = slug === 'home' ? 'home' : slug let pageConfigPath = slug if (slug === 'home') pageConfigPath = 'home' try { pageConfig = JSON.parse(readFileSync(join(REPO, 'nexa-pages', `${pageConfigPath}.json`), 'utf-8')) } catch {} ``` Also remove the `return null` on catch — home page shouldn't 404 on config load failure. **Detection:** ```bash python3 -c "import json; exec(open('src/lib/page-data.ts').read().split('export')[1]); d=loadPageData('es','home'); print('sections:', len(d.get('pageConfig',{}).get('sections',[])))" ``` Should show section count, not 0. **The pattern:** Any `if (slug !== 'home')` guard that skips config loading for the home page is a systematic bug. The home page IS a page in `nexa-pages/home.json` and its `sections` array must be loaded like any other page. The original author likely assumed the home page uses a hardcoded layout, but in config-driven sites, it uses the same section dispatch as all other pages. ### Images Not Resolving: images.json Wrapper Structure **Symptom:** Site loads, sections render with text content, but `<img>` elements have empty `src`. No image errors in console because `resolveImage()` returns empty string. **Root cause:** Two issues stack: 1. `loadJSON('images.json')` returns the full file: `{ tenant, basePath, images: { hero: {...}, trust: {...} } }` 2. `resolveImage(images, "@img:hero.localizedEs")` traverses `images.hero.localizedEs.src` — but the passed `images` is the **wrapper**, not the inner `images` key **Fix in page.tsx:** Pass `data.images.images` instead of `data.images`: ```tsx // Before: renders empty img tags <SectionsRenderer images={data.images} /> // After: resolves actual image paths <SectionsRenderer images={data.images.images} /> ``` Apply to ALL page.tsx files that pass images to SectionsRenderer: - `src/app/[locale]/page.tsx` (home) - `src/app/[locale]/[slug]/page.tsx` (all other pages) **Verification:** ```bash curl -sL https://site.com/es/ | grep -oP '/images/hero/[^\"'\''&]+\.webp' | sort -u # Should show resolved image paths, not empty src ``` ## Troubleshooting Checklist When a Next.js site shows 404 or doesn't serve content: 1. [ ] Check build output — are the expected routes listed? 2. [ ] Check `routes-manifest.json` — are routes registered? 3. [ ] Check `page.js` files — are they compiled? 4. [ ] Test HTTP inside container — `node -e` with raw TCP connection 5. [ ] Check `content/` and page configuration files exist in image 6. [ ] For GitHub Packages: check `.npmrc` was copied to Docker build cache 7. [ ] For standalone: check server.js runs without EADDRINUSE 8. [ ] Verify Traefik labels point to correct service port (3000) 9. [ ] Verify Traefik labels use correct Docker network 10. [ ] Check Cloudflare is proxying to VPS origin (not cached page) ", "path": "software-development/nextjs-deployment-debugging/SKILL.md", "skill_dir": "/root/.hermes/skills/software-development/nextjs-deployment-debugging", "linked_files": {"references": ["references/[REDACTED_TOKEN].md", "references/nexa-ppr-content-debugging.md", "references/turbopack-file-link-symlink-resolution.md", "references/ssr-content-duplication-debugging.md", "references/private-npm-docker-workflow.md", "references/component-architecture-pattern.md", "references/dist-src-divergence-nexa-[REDACTED_PHONE].md", "references/nexa-debugging-transcript.md", "references/nextjs-section-content-pipeline.md"]}, "usage_hint": "To view linked files, call skill_view(name, file_path) where file_path is e.g. 'references/api.md' or 'assets/config.yaml'", "required_environment_variables": [], "required_commands": [], "missing_required_environment_variables": [], "missing_credential_files": [], "missing_required_commands": [], "setup_needed": false, "setup_skipped": false, "readiness_status": "available", "metadata": {"hermes": {"tags": ["nextjs", "deployment", "docker", "debugging", "build", "routes", "pages-router", "app-router"], "related_skills": ["systematic-debugging", "ecommerce-codebase-audit", "kanban-orchestrator"]}}}

messages.content:
{"success": true, "name": "ecommerce-codebase-audit", "description": "Audit, navigate, and iterate on a Next.js e-commerce project (admin panel + storefront). Covers layout patterns, theme system, visual asset inventory/cleanup, image gap analysis, auth cookie debugging, deployment follow-through, and post-deployment client readiness review.", "tags": ["nextjs", "ecommerce", "admin", "audit", "tailwind", "layout", "sidebar", "supabase", "auth", "cloudflare", "org-restructuring", "package-extraction"], "related_skills": [], "content": "--- name: ecommerce-codebase-audit description: "Audit, navigate, and iterate on a Next.js e-commerce project (admin panel + storefront). Covers layout patterns, theme system, visual asset inventory/cleanup, image gap analysis, auth cookie debugging, deployment follow-through, and post-deployment client readiness review." version: 1.5.0 tags: [nextjs, ecommerce, admin, audit, tailwind, layout, sidebar, supabase, auth, cloudflare, org-restructuring, package-extraction] --- # Ecommerce Codebase Audit Inspect and improve a Next.js e-commerce admin panel. Focus on layout consistency, theme application, page structure, image assets, and auth flow. ## Audit Checklist ### Layout Consistency - [ ] Every admin page wraps content in `AdminShell` from `@/components/admin/admin-layout` - [ ] `AdminShell` includes the sidebar navigation (responsive: hidden on mobile, fixed on desktop) - [ ] Sidebar uses the same links and active-state highlighting across all pages - [ ] All pages render inside `` not just individually ### Theme System The admin theme uses emerald/zinc on a dark background (#0a0a0b / #0f0f10): ```tsx // Tailwind classes to look for bg-[#0f0f10] // sidebar + card backgrounds bg-zinc-800/60 // borders text-emerald-400 // active text bg-emerald-500/10 // active background text-zinc-400 // inactive text hover:bg-zinc-800/40 // hover state ``` - [ ] All pages use the same color tokens (not hardcoded different colors) - [ ] Active page shows `bg-emerald-500/10 text-emerald-400` with indicator dot - [ ] Inactive pages use `text-zinc-400 hover:text-zinc-200 hover:bg-zinc-800/40` ### Page Structure Each admin page follows this pattern: ```tsx 'use client' import { AdminShell, useAdminAuth } from "@/components/admin/admin-layout" import { useState, useEffect } from "react" export default function PageName() { const { authed } = useAdminAuth() if (!authed) return null return ( <>

Page Title

{/* content */} ) } ``` - [ ] Pages use `{ authed } = useAdminAuth()` for auth guard - [ ] Pages use `AdminShell` wrapper (already in the root layout) - [ ] Pages use `fetch("/api/admin/*")` for data (not direct Supabase client) - [ ] Data fetched via `/api/admin/` routes that use service_role key server-side ### AdminShell Component Located in `@/components/admin/admin-layout` (or wherever it's imported from). The AdminShell provides: - Sidebar navigation with 4 groups: Operaciones, Catálogo, Marketing, Configuración - Mobile responsiveness (hidden sidebar on mobile, toggle with hamburger) - Auth guard via `useAdminAuth` - Consistent background and layout padding ## Auth Flow & Cookie Debugging ### The Core Trap: Two Auth Paths Crossing In Next.js + Supabase e-commerce projects, there are usually TWO auth paths that can conflict: **Path A — Consumer auth (useAuth):** - Login page calls `supabase.auth.signInWithPassword()` directly from the browser client - Supabase's `@supabase/ssr` browser client sets the session cookie automatically - `/mi-cuenta` reads session via `supabase.auth.getSession()` — works because cookie exists **Path B — Admin auth (useAdminAuth / /api/auth):** - Login page calls `/api/auth` with `action: "login"` - API route creates an SSR client with `createServerClient()`, but often has `setAll() {}` empty - **The session cookie never gets set on the response** — the API returns tokens, but no cookie - Admin layout falls back to localStorage or custom cookie **Common failure modes:** 1. **Login works (gets tokens) but /mi-cuenta shows unauthenticated** — consumer login page fetches `/api/auth` instead of calling Supabase directly. Fix: use `useAuth().login()` (Supabase browser client) which sets the correct session cookie. 2. **Login works but /admin redirects back to /login** — admin layout's `useAdminAuth()` calls `/api/auth?action=me` with a token from localStorage, but the API route's token verification path fails. Fix: ensure the API route can verify tokens against Supabase REST API (not just SSR cookies). ### Fix Checklist - [ ] Consumer login uses Supabase browser client directly (`supabase.auth.signInWithPassword()`), NOT a custom `/api/auth` endpoint - [ ] After successful login, store session tokens in `localStorage` for admin layout fallback: `localStorage.setItem("elviajero_admin_session", JSON.stringify({ access_token, refresh_token }))` - [ ] Admin layout checks localStorage first, then Supabase SSR cookie, then custom cookie — triple fallback - [ ] API route `action: "me"` verifies tokens via `fetch(${supabaseUrl}/auth/v1/user)` not `supabase.auth.getUser()` - [ ] Service-role client for profile queries uses `createClient(url, serviceRoleKey)` — passing service key as second arg, not in headers - [ ] Role check queries DB profiles table (not JWT claim) — `profiles.role` not `user.role` ### Debugging Auth Issues ```bash # Check if API route is reachable curl -sX POST https://el-viajero.paragu-ai.com/api/auth \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"action":"login","email":"[REDACTED_EMAIL]","password":"test"}' | head -c 200 # Check if session cookie is set curl -sv https://el-viajero.paragu-ai.com/mi-cuenta 2>&1 | grep -i 'set-cookie' # Check server logs docker service logs elviajero-comercio_web --tail 30 --no-task-ids ``` ## Visual Asset Workflow ### Full Asset Overhaul Pattern When doing a complete visual assets redesign (inventory, generation, deployment): #### Phase 1: Inventory ```bash # List all files on disk find public/images -type f | sort # List all code references grep -rohP '"/images/[^"'"'"']+' --include='*.{tsx,ts,json,css}' | sed 's/^"//' | sort -u # Cross-reference: 3 outcomes per image # 1. Referenced + on disk = OK # 2. Referenced + missing = FIX (add file) # 3. On disk + no reference = ORPHAN (delete) # Size audit — small = programmatic (low quality) find public/images -name "*.png" -size -50k ``` #### Phase 2: Categorization | Type | Size threshold | Keep as | Upgrade? | |------|---------------|---------|----------| | SVGs | varies | Vector | Only if they're emoji text | | Real photos | >50KB | PNG | No | | Programmatic renders | <50KB | PNG | Yes — regenerate with richer Pillow composites | | Icons | 4-8KB | PNG | Intentionally small — minimalist silhouettes | | Hero backgrounds | <20KB | PNG | Yes — add atmosphere, silhouettes, gradients | #### Phase 3: Generation (no AI API available) Use Pillow for progressive enhancement rounds: **Round 1 — Placeholders:** ```python # Sky gradient + bokeh circles + category silhouettes # Write to a generate script, deploy, iterate ``` **Round 2 — Richer details:** - Tree silhouettes, stars, glowing tent, moon - River reflections, fishing rod, sun at horizon - Mountain trails, hiker with backpack - Headlight beams, road lines, car approaching - Dust clouds, motorcycle, fence - Fields, barn, sun, clouds **Round 3 — AI (if REPLICATE_API_TOKEN or equivalent available):** ```python if call_replicate(prompt, w, h, path): print("AI generated") else: render_fallback(path) # keep best Pillow version ``` **Round 4 — Self-critique:** After deploying, review each asset and write a brutal critique doc. Then regenerate the worst offenders in the next round. #### Phase 4: Deployment Pitfalls **Cloudflare cache (IMPORTANT):** - CF sets `cache-control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable` — 1 YEAR cache - No CF API key? Only option is **rename the file** (logo.png → logo-v2.png) - Then update ALL code references to point to new filename - Verify with curl, not browser: `curl -sk URL/images/logo-v2.png -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code} %{size_download}B"` - Then tell the user to hard refresh (Cmd+Shift+R) **Docker layer cache:** - If files exist locally but return 404 on deploy, the Docker COPY was cached - Fix: `docker build --no-cache` or touch a file in the COPY layer **Verify the right file is served:** ```bash # Check HTML references curl -sk https://example.com/ | grep -oP '(logo|favicon)-v2' # Check file directly curl -sk https://example.com/images/logo-v2.png -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code} %{size_download}B" ``` ### Key Project-Specific Patterns (El Viajero) - Product images: `content/es.json` → `/images/marketing/products/*.png` (34 products) - Category backgrounds (dynamic SVG): `/images/categories/${slug}.svg` — slug values in `app/categoria/*/page.tsx` - Category heroes: heroImage prop → `app/categoria/*/page.tsx` → `/images/marketing/hero-*.png` - Hero carousel: content-driven array in `content/es.json` → `/images/marketing/hero-carousel-*.png` - Testimonials: `"image"` field in `content/es.json` → `/images/testimonials/avatar-*.png` - Logo: `header.tsx` → `/images/logo.png` (or `logo-v2.png` after cache-bust) - Favicon: `layout.tsx` → `/images/favicon.png` (or `favicon-v2.png`) - OG image: `layout.tsx` → `/images/og-viajero.svg` - PWA icons: `manifest.json` → `/images/icon-192.png`, `/images/icon-512.png` ### Generation Recipes **Category icons (128x128):** Create a Python script with `ImageDraw` — tent, fish, umbrella, backpack, car, motorcycle, barn silhouettes on green circle background. These are intentionally small (4-8KB). **Hero banners (1920x600):** Create scene composites: 1. Sky gradient (top to bottom, warm/cool depending on scene) 2. Ground layer (dark rectangle) 3. Scene elements (tree silhouettes, stars, moon, tent, river, fishing rod, mountains, road, headlights, barn, fence, sun) 4. Bokeh overlay (random circles with low alpha) 5. Gaussian blur (radius 0.3-0.5) for polish **Testimonial avatars (200x200):** If no AI available, create colored circles with basic face features (eyes, smile, hair shape, shirt). Tell the client these are placeholders for real customer photos. **PWA icons:** Render from the logo at 192x192 and 512x512 with anti-aliasing. ## Multi-Locale Content Updates (PAB Sites) When updating content across all locales (en/es/nl/de) in a PAB (Paragu-AI Builder) project: ### Safe approach vs. pitfalls 1. **Python JSON manipulation is safer than `patch` tool for large content changes.** The `patch` tool works well for small string replacements, but when replacing JSON blocks spanning 50+ lines, it can match the wrong text, leave orphaned content, and produce valid-but-wrong JSON. This happened when replacing the `processPage` section — patch matched a subset, leaving trailing orphan that was syntactically valid but structurally wrong. 2. **When `patch` goes wrong: stop, don't stack.** If the first patch produces unexpected results (orphan content, duplicated sections, lost keys), do NOT apply more patches on top. Switch immediately to Python `json.load()/json.dump()` — read the broken file, fix the data structure in memory, and write it back clean. Stacking patches compounds the mistakes and makes the file increasingly fragile to further edits. The `processPage` fix required three patches to clean up what one wrong match did. 3. **`patch` can match content from a previously-applied patch** — If a file was already modified, the old_string might not match what's actually in the file. Always read the file fresh before patching, and verify with a direct key inspection after (`python3 -c "import json; d=json.load(open('f')); print(d.get('targetKey',{}).keys())"`). 4. **Shell variable expansion fails in `execute_code`'s multi-line Python strings** — `for f in $FILES; do python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('$f'))"; done` breaks because `$f` isn't expanded inside the Python string. Use explicit file paths, a temp script, or Python's own `glob` module instead. 5. **Always validate JSON structure after content changes, not just syntax.** A file can be valid JSON but structurally wrong (missing keys, wrong step counts, orphan content). After validation: ```bash python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('file'))" # syntax check ``` Also verify structural integrity: ```python d["processPage"]["process"]["steps"] # should have 5 steps d["home"]["services"]["groups"] # should have 3 groups d["aboutPage"]["story"]["paragraphs"] # should have 3 paragraphs ``` 6. **When deleting orphaned content from a botched patch, read the file with JSON parser first**, then find the structural key that contains the orphan data. Delete it from the Python dict and re-write. Don't try to surgically remove lines with patch. 7. **Process step consistency rule:** The same process narrative lives in 6 places across the site. Update ALL of these together in the same script block — missing any one creates inconsistency: - `home.process.steps` (homepage) - `processPage.process.steps` (process page) - `landingInversor.process.steps`, `landingTrust.process.steps`, `landingLifestyle.process.steps`, `landingEmpresa.process.steps` (LinkedIn ad landing pages) 8. **Process for adding new content sections across locales:** - Step 1: Define the content data structure in a Python dict with all localizations - Step 2: Load each locale file, apply transformations, write back - Step 3: Validate JSON syntax for all files - Step 4: Verify structural keys match expectations (count groups, steps, paragraphs) ```python for lang in ["en", "es", "nl", "de"]: with open(f"content/{lang}.json") as f: d = json.load(f) d["parentSection"]["newKey"] = translations[lang] with open(f"content/{lang}.json", "w") as f: json.dump(d, f, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False) ``` 9. **Section structure for the 'Our Services' pattern:** ```python data["home"]["services"] = { "eyebrow": "Our Services", "title": "Section title", "groups": [ {"id": "group-id", "title": "Group Name", "subtitle": "Group subtitle.", "items": [{"title": "Service", "description": "Desc.", "icon": "IconName"}]} ] } ``` 10. **Section structure for the 'Story' pattern:** ```python data["aboutPage"]["story"] = { "title": "Our Story", "eyebrow": "Tagline", "paragraphs": ["Para 1.", "Para 2.", "Para 3."] } ``` 11. **Section structure for the 'Process steps' pattern:** ```python data["processPage"]["process"]["steps"] = [ {"number": 1, "title": "Step Name", "description": "Long description.", "icon": "IconName", "image": {"$img": "process.key"}, "duration": "Week 1"} ] ``` 12. **Landing page Calendly URL pattern:** ```python base = "https://calendly.com/nexaparaguay/consulta" utm = f"utm_source=linkedin&utm_medium=paid&utm_campaign={campaign}" url = f"{base}?{utm}&utm_content=cta-{placement}-{lang}" ``` ## Deployment ### Docker Deployment After modifying content assets (images, content JSON, or static files): 1. **Rebuild** — `npm run build` catches missing images and type errors 2. **Rebuild Docker image** — `docker build -t elviajero-comercio:prod .` 3. **Deploy** — `docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml elviajero-comercio --detach=false` 4. **Verify** — `curl -sI https://el-viajero.paragu-ai.com/ | head -5` 5. **Force rolling update** — if same image tag, `docker service update --force elviajero-comercio_web` Never assume changes to static content or images are live — Docker rebuild and stack deploy are required. Browser cache is NOT the server — always curl first. ### Production Monitoring After every deploy, add: - Healthcheck endpoint at `/api/health` returning `{status, timestamp, uptime}` - Docker HEALTHCHECK in Dockerfile (every 30s, 40s start grace, 3 retries) - Cron healthcheck via Hermes: every 15m, auto-redeploy on failure - Shell check script: `/root/scripts/check-{project}.sh` ### Docker Build Timeout `docker build` with npm install can take >120s on cold cache. Set terminal timeout to 300s for builds. If it times out, retry — the npm cache is warm on the second run and it will complete faster. #### Next.js `pages/` Folder Conflict (v16+) Next.js 16+ errors with "`pages` and `app` directories should be under the same folder" when a `pages/` data directory exists at project root alongside `src/app/`. Fix: 1. Rename `pages/` to `nexa-pages/` 2. Update all `page.tsx` references from `'pages'` to `'nexa-pages'` 3. Update Dockerfile to copy `nexa-pages/` into the standalone output 4. Run `docker build --no-cache` after rename (Docker layer cache may use old path) #### Docker Standalone + Data Files Next.js `output: "standalone"` produces a `server.js` that does `process.chdir(__dirname)` — so `process.cwd()` points to `.next/standalone/`, not the app root. Data files read at runtime via `readFileSync(path.join(process.cwd(), ...))` must be copied into `.next/standalone/`: ```dockerfile COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./ COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/nexa-pages ./.next/standalone/nexa-pages COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/content ./.next/standalone/content ``` Without this, the container runs fine but returns static HTML with fallback data because `readFileSync` silently fails and the catch block returns defaults. #### The "Welcome" Bug (Static SSG Fallback) When a PAB page always renders "Welcome" regardless of content changes, the root cause is in the page component's fallback chain: ```tsx // BROKEN: home.json has 'titleKey' not 'title' — always falls through {homePage.title || content?.title || 'Welcome'} // FIXED: use siteName from the locale JSON {content?.siteName || 'Nexa Paraguay'} ``` Check that `getPages()` and `getContent()` actually read files via `readFileSync`/`readdirSync` at build time. The `catch` block silently swallowing the error is the symptom — verify the files are accessible during the Docker build stage. #### `window.gtag` Type Conflicts When a project uses `declare global { interface Window { gtag?: any } }` and imports from `@ai-whisperers/seo/analytics` (which also declares it), you get: ``` Type error: All declarations of 'gtag' must have identical modifiers. ``` **Fix:** Use `(window as any).gtag` everywhere instead of declaring `interface Window`: ```typescript const w = window as any w.gtag("event", "page_view", data) ``` Apply to all GA4 tracking files: `components/analytics.tsx`, `lib/analytics.ts`. ## Traefik + Docker Swarm Routing Setup When deploying a new Next.js client site to an existing Docker Swarm + Traefik infrastructure (paragu-ai.com VPS at 72.61.44.159): ### 1. DNS Record (Cloudflare) Add an A record in the Cloudflare dashboard for the paragu-ai.com zone: ``` Type: A Name: {site-name} Content: 72.61.44.159 Proxy status: Proxied (orange cloud) ``` ### 2. docker-compose.yml (Traefik Labels) The template's `docker-compose.yml` has superspuma's labels. Update them: ```yaml services: web: build: . image: {site-name}:prod ports: ["3000"] networks: [agent-net] deploy: mode: replicated replicas: 2 resources: limits: { memory: 512M, cpus: '0.5' } reservations: { memory: 128M, cpus: '0.1' } labels: - "traefik.enable=true" - "traefik.http.routers.{site-name}.entrypoints=websecure" - "traefik.http.routers.{site-name}.rule=Host(`{site-name}.paragu-ai.com`)" - "traefik.http.routers.{site-name}.tls=true" - "traefik.http.routers.{site-name}.tls.certresolver=letsencryptresolver" - "traefik.http.services.{site-name}.loadbalancer.server.port=3000" - "traefik.docker.network=agent-net" networks: agent-net: external: true ``` Pitfall: the `image` field in docker-compose.yml must match the `docker build -t` tag. ### 3. Deploy (Manual on VPS) ```bash ssh root@72.61.44.159 cd /root/{repo-name} git fetch origin && git reset --hard origin/main export GITHUB_TOKEN=$(gh auth token) # for @ai-whisperers npm packages npm ci --legacy-peer-deps npm run build docker build -t {site-name}:prod . docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml {site-name} ``` After deploy, verify: ```bash curl -sI https://{site-name}.paragu-ai.com/ | grep "HTTP/" ``` ### 4. Docker Build GITHUB_TOKEN Issue The standard Docker multi-stage build (template's Dockerfile) runs `npm install` inside the build container but doesn't have access to the GITHUB_TOKEN for @ai-whisperers GitHub Packages registry. **Two solutions:** **Option A (simple):** Build on the host, then use a production-only Dockerfile that copies the already-built output: ```dockerfile # Dockerfile.prod — used after `npm run build` on host FROM node:20-alpine AS runner WORKDIR /app ENV NODE_ENV=production NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1 PORT=3000 HOSTNAME="0.0.0.0" RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs && adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs COPY --chown=nextjs:nodejs . . USER nextjs EXPOSE 3000 CMD ["node_modules/.bin/next", "start"] ``` ```bash # On VPS: npm run build # host build has GITHUB_TOKEN docker build -f Dockerfile.prod -t site:prod . # just ships node_modules + .next docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml site ``` **Option B (Docker build-arg):** Pass the token as a build arg and set it in the .npmrc at build time: ```dockerfile FROM node:20-alpine AS deps WORKDIR /app COPY package.json package-lock.json* .npmrc ./ ARG NODE_AUTH_TOKEN RUN echo "//npm.pkg.github.com/:_authToken=${NODE_AUTH_TOKEN}" >> .npmrc && \ npm install --legacy-peer-deps ``` ```bash docker build --build-arg NODE_AUTH_TOKEN=$(gh auth token) -t site:prod . ``` **Pitfall:** Docker BuildKit blocks ARG values from ENV for security ("Do not use ARG or ENV instructions for sensitive data"). Option A is more reliable. ### 5. CI/CD Workflow The reusable ci-cd workflows (Ai-Whisperers/ci-cd) may not be accessible from new repos due to permissions. Example standalone deploy.yml as fallback: ```yaml name: Deploy on: push: branches: [main] jobs: deploy: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - uses: actions/setup-node@v4 with: node-version: '20' cache: 'npm' - run: npm ci --legacy-peer-deps - run: npm run build - name: Setup SSH run: | mkdir -p ~/.ssh echo "${{ secrets.VPS_KEY }}" > ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 ssh-keyscan -H ${{ secrets.VPS_HOST }} >> ~/.ssh/known_hosts 2>/dev/null || true - name: Deploy via SSH run: | ssh ${{ secrets.VPS_USER }}@${{ secrets.VPS_HOST }} \ "cd /root/{repo-name} && git fetch origin && git reset --hard origin/main && \ export GITHUB_TOKEN=$(gh auth token) && npm ci --legacy-peer-deps && npm run build && \ docker build -f Dockerfile.prod -t {site-name}:prod . && \ docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml {site-name}" ``` ## Google Maps Browser Navigation for Search-Engine-Blocked Research When search engines (Google, DuckDuckGo, Bing, Brave) block automated queries with CAPTCHAs/IP blocks, use browser_navigate to Google Maps as a workaround: ### Pattern 1. Navigate to Google Maps search URL directly: ```bash https://www.google.com/maps/search/{query}+{location} ``` 2. The browser snapshot shows results as readable `article` elements with: - Company name (clickable) - Rating (stars) - Address - Phone number - Hours - Website link (when available) 3. Click a result to view more details in the side panel. 4. To refine, clear the combobox and type a new query, then press Enter. ### Queries That Work Spanish-language queries work better for Paraguayan businesses: ``` "constructora civil Asuncion Paraguay" "vidrio templado laminado fachada Asuncion" "fachada vidrio aluminio Asuncion Paraguay" "desarrolladora inmobiliaria Asuncion Paraguay" "arquitecto Asuncion Paraguay fachada" ``` ### Limitations - Only shows ~7 results per view (Google Maps limited view without login) - No API access to extract full data programmatically - Phone numbers and emails must be copied manually from snapshots - Results are biased toward Google's ranking, not comprehensive - Works for Paraguay because Google Maps has good coverage there ## B2B Lead-Gen Conversion (e-commerce template → B2B site) When converting the e-commerce template (template-nextjs-client) into a B2B lead-generation site (construction materials, industrial supply, wholesale), follow this pattern: ### What to REMOVE from the template ``` B2C pages to delete: app/tienda/ → product store with cart/checkout app/envio/ → shipping info app/promociones/ → promotions app/producto/[slug]/ → individual product pages (replace with /productos/ catalog) B2C components to delete: search-autocomplete.tsx → no product search for B2B dark-mode-toggle.tsx → B2B stays light theme payment-methods.tsx → no payments on lead-gen site quick-order.tsx → no quick ordering product-card.tsx → replace with spec-card pattern hero-carousel.tsx → use static hero for B2B promo-carousel.tsx → no promos bottom-nav.tsx → not needed for B2B mobile empty-state.tsx → no shopping experience recently-viewed.tsx → no browsing history wishlist.ts / lib/ → no wishlists ``` ### Post-Deployment Content Audit / Roast Pattern After deploying a converted B2B site (or any new client site from template), run a systematic content audit by browsing every page live and checking for: 1. **Template remnants**: Any text/images from the original template site that leaked through (Fun4Me brand, superspuma references, wrong Instagram handles). Check /contacto, /nosotros, /faq, footer especially — these are the most commonly overlooked. 2. **Content that doesn't match the business type**: - B2B construction site still showing e-commerce order tracking - Professional services site saying "bienestar íntimo" (sex shop) - Law firm showing shipping/returns information 3. **Placeholder phone numbers**: Any hardcoded `WA_PHONE = "[REDACTED_PHONE]"` (Fun4Me's number) that wasn't replaced. 4. **Broken links**: Links pointing to removed pages (/tienda, /envio, /producto/[slug]). 5. **Empty portfolio/projects**: "Coming soon" or "próximamente" on a B2B site that needs trust signals. Replace with a CTA-driven alternative. ### Content Audit / Roast Methodology Browser every page on the live site using browser_navigate and check: | Page | Common Issues | |------|---------------| | /contacto | Fun4Me Instagram, returns/orders/shipping links, wrong phone | | /nosotros | "¿Por qué Fun4Me?" section, sex shop story, purple heart, /tienda links | | /faq | Generic questions instead of industry-specific technical answers | | /proyectos | "Coming soon" instead of real portfolio | | /productos | Emoji icons instead of real product images | | /blog | No images, missing headers, broken formatting | After audit, fix the 3 worst pages (usually contacto, nosotros, faq) in one commit, then re-deploy and verify. ### Post-Deployment Content Audit / Roast Pattern After deploying a converted B2B site (or any new client site from template), run a systematic content audit by browsing every page live and checking for: 1. **Template remnants**: Any text/images from the original template site that leaked through (Fun4Me brand, superspuma references, wrong Instagram handles). Check /contacto, /nosotros, /faq, footer especially — these are the most commonly overlooked. 2. **Content that doesn't match the business type**: - B2B construction site still showing e-commerce order tracking - Professional services site saying "bienestar íntimo" (sex shop) - Law firm showing shipping/returns information 3. **Placeholder phone numbers**: Any hardcoded `WA_PHONE = "[REDACTED_PHONE]"` (Fun4Me's number) that wasn't replaced. 4. **Broken links**: Links pointing to removed pages (/tienda, /envio, /producto/[slug]). 5. **Empty portfolio/projects**: "Coming soon" or "próximamente" on a B2B site that needs trust signals. Replace with a CTA-driven alternative. ### Content Audit / Roast Methodology Browser every page on the live site using browser_navigate and check: | Page | Common Issues | |------|---------------| | /contacto | Fun4Me Instagram, returns/orders/shipping links, wrong phone | | /nosotros | "¿Por qué Fun4Me?" section, sex shop story, purple heart, /tienda links | | /faq | Generic questions instead of industry-specific technical answers | | /proyectos | "Coming soon" instead of real portfolio | | /productos | Emoji icons instead of real product images | | /blog | No images, missing headers, broken formatting | After audit, fix the 3 worst pages (usually contacto, nosotros, faq) in one commit, then re-deploy and verify. ### Full list of Fun4Me artifacts to hunt when converting template When converting the template to a B2B site, these Fun4Me-specific traces survive even after page/component deletion: | File | What to fix | |------|-------------| | `app/contacto/page.tsx` | Hardcoded `WA_PHONE = "[REDACTED_PHONE]"`, Instagram `@fun4me_py`, "Estado del pedido" / "Reclamo / devolución" quick links | | `app/nosotros/page.tsx` | `brand.story` fallback to "bienestar íntimo con total privacidad" (sex shop), "¿Por qué Fun4Me?" section, purple heart emoji, `/tienda` link | | `app/sitemap.ts` | Hardcoded `base = "https://fun4me.paragu-ai.com"`, fun4me page paths | | `content/es.json` | `analytics.ga4` empty string (needs real GA4 ID), `home.hero.ctaPrimaryHref: "/tienda"` | | `layout.tsx` | Theme-color `#1B5E20` (green, not blue B2B), metadata title fallbacks | | `components/` | {"success": true, "name": "ecommerce-codebase-audit", "file": "references/el-viajero-admin-panel.md", "content": "# El Viajero Admin Panel Reference

## Project Path
/root/elviajero-comercio

## Admin Pages (18)

| Page | Route | State | Has Search | Has Pagination | Uses Shared Components |
|------|-------|-------|-----------|----------------|----------------------|
| Dashboard | /admin | ✅ Tier 1 | — | — | StatCard (inline), StatsGridSkeleton |
| Pedidos | /admin/pedidos | ✅ Tier 1 | ✅ SearchInput | ✅ 20/page | PageHeader, SearchInput, FilterBar, SummaryBar, OrderCard, CardSkeleton, EmptyState |
| Clientes | /admin/clientes | ✅ Tier 1 | ✅ SearchInput | — | PageHeader, SearchInput, StatCard, Badge, CardSkeleton, EmptyState |
| Reportes | /admin/reportes | ✅ Tier 1 | — | — | PageHeader, StatCard, EmptyState, StatsGridSkeleton, CSV export |
| Productos | /admin/productos | ✅ Tier 1 | ✅ SearchInput | ✅ 20/page | PageHeader, SearchInput, ImageUpload, image preview modal |
| Categorías | /admin/categorias | ✅ Tier 1 | ✅ SearchInput | — | PageHeader, SearchInput, EmptyState, StatsGridSkeleton |
| Enriquecer | /admin/enriquecer | ✅ Tier 1 | — | — | PageHeader, AI enrich button (✨ IA) |
| Fotos | /admin/fotos | 🟡 Tier 2 | — | — | ImageUpload inline |
| Importar | /admin/importar | ✅ Tier 1 | — | — | Template download, preview table, bulk import |
| Blog | /admin/blog | 🟡 Tier 2 | — | — | Full CRUD, publish toggle |
| Promos | /admin/promos | ✅ Tier 1 | ✅ SearchInput | — | PageHeader, SearchInput, EmptyState, TableSkeleton, edit + date expiration |
| Reseñas | /admin/resenas | 🟡 Tier 2 | — | — | Product filter pills, star rating |
| B2B | /admin/b2b | 🟡 Tier 2 | — | — | CRUD table, status toggle |
| Contenido | /admin/contenido | ✅ Tier 1 | — | — | Deep content editor with overrides (10 sections: general, hero, about, contacto, footer, faq, testimonials, stats, features, seo) |
| Usuarios | /admin/usuarios | ✅ Tier 1 | ✅ SearchInput | — | PageHeader, SearchInput, DataTable, Badge, EmptyState, TableSkeleton |
| Suscriptores | /admin/suscriptores | ✅ Tier 1 | — | — | PageHeader, EmptyState, TableSkeleton, date column, copy emails |
| Tema | /admin/tema | ✅ Tier 1 | — | — | PageHeader, 5 presets, DB persistence via /api/admin/theme, localStorage fallback |

## Components
@/components/admin/admin-layout      — AdminShell + useAdminAuth
@/components/admin/sidebar            — AdminSidebar (4 groups: Operaciones, Catálogo, Marketing, Configuración)
@/components/admin/ui                 — 16 reusable components (StatCard, EmptyState, CardSkeleton, TableSkeleton, StatsGridSkeleton, FilterBar, SearchInput, PageHeader, Badge, StatusSelect, SummaryBar, OrderCard, DataTable)
@/components/admin/image-upload       — ImageUpload (Supabase Storage)

## Data Fetching Pattern
All admin pages call fetch("/api/admin/...") — NOT direct Supabase.
API routes (/app/api/admin/*/route.ts) use getAdminClient() from @/lib/supabase/admin (service_role key).

## API Routes
/api/admin/stats       — Dashboard stats (GET)
/api/admin/orders      — Orders CRUD (GET, PATCH)
/api/admin/customers   — Customers list (GET)
/api/admin/products    — Products CRUD (GET, PATCH, DELETE, POST)
/api/admin/products/bulk — Bulk product import (POST)
/api/admin/categories  — Categories CRUD (GET, POST, DELETE)
/api/admin/users       — Users list (GET)
/api/admin/subscribers — Subscribers list (GET)
/api/admin/blog        — Blog CRUD (GET, POST, PATCH, DELETE)
/api/admin/promos      — Promo codes CRUD (GET, POST, PATCH, DELETE)
/api/admin/b2b         — B2B customers (GET, POST, PATCH)
/api/admin/reviews     — Reviews (GET, DELETE)
/api/admin/content     — Content overrides (GET, POST)
/api/admin/theme       — Theme persistence (GET, POST)
/api/admin/enrich      — AI product enrichment (POST)

## Auth Pattern
```tsx
'use client'
import { AdminShell, useAdminAuth } from "@/components/admin/admin-layout"
const { authed } = useAdminAuth()
if (!authed) return null
```

Login stores session in localStorage + elviajero_admin_token cookie.
useAdminAuth tries localStorage → Supabase SSR cookie → custom cookie, all via /api/auth with Authorization: Bearer header.

## Theme Colors
- Page bg: #0a0a0b
- Sidebar bg: #0f0f10
- Border: zinc-800/60
- Active link: emerald-500/10 bg, emerald-400 text, indicator dot
- Inactive link: zinc-400 text, hover zinc-200 / zinc-800/40 bg
- Headings: white
- Cards: bg-zinc-900/50 with border zinc-800/60
- Labels: zinc-500 text, 10px, uppercase tracking-wider

## Deployment
```bash
cd /root/elviajero-comercio
npm run build
docker build -t elviajero-comercio:prod .
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml elviajero-comercio --detach=false
docker service update --force elviajero-comercio_web   # if image tag unchanged
```
